subject: Chemistry
text: Classification of Alcohols: Containg sp 3 C-OH bond -CH 2 -OH(1 0 ), >CH-OH(2 0 ), Allylic: CH 2 =CH-CH 2- OH3 Benzylic: Containg sp 2 C-OH bond-Vinylic CH 2 =CH-OH Phenols:
text_html:
Classification of Alcohols:

  1. Containg sp3C-OH bond
    1. -CH2-OH(10),
    2. >CH-OH(20),

    3. Allylic: CH2=CH-CH2-OH3
    4. Benzylic: 
  2. Containg sp2 C-OH bond-Vinylic
    1. CH2=CH-OH
    2. Phenols:

subject: Chemistry
text: Nomenclature: Alcohols: Common names (Alkyl group + alcohol) IUPAC (Alkan+ol, substituting -e by - ol e.g. ethanol) Phenols: Common names (as derivatives of phenol with position like ortho-, meta-, para-) IUPAC (Derivatives of phenols with numbers like 1,2-, 1,3-e.g. 2-Nitrophenol or o-nitrophenol) Ethers: Common names (alkyl/aryl groups in alphabetical order followed by ether e.g. ethyl methyl ether) IUPAC (In alkyl/aryl group-e is replaced by oxy followed by parent hydrocarbon e.g. methoxyethane)
text_html:
Nomenclature:
Alcohols: Common names (Alkyl group + alcohol)
IUPAC (Alkan+ol, substituting -e by - ol e.g. ethanol)
Phenols: Common names (as derivatives of phenol with position like ortho-, meta-, para-)
IUPAC (Derivatives of phenols with numbers like 1,2-, 1,3-e.g. 2-Nitrophenol or o-nitrophenol)
Ethers: Common names (alkyl/aryl groups in alphabetical order followed by ether e.g. ethyl methyl ether) IUPAC (In alkyl/aryl group-e is replaced by oxy followed by parent hydrocarbon e.g. methoxyethane)

subject: Chemistry
text: Common Naming of Alcohols The common name of an alcohol comes from the alkyl group joined with "alcohol." Example: CH 3 OH is methyl alcohol.
text_html:
Common Naming of Alcohols
The common name of an alcohol comes from the alkyl group joined with "alcohol." Example: CH3OH is methyl alcohol.

subject: Chemistry
text: IUPAC Naming System IUPAC names use the alkane name and replace 'e' with 'ol'. Example: Methane becomes methanol.
text_html:
IUPAC Naming System
IUPAC names use the alkane name and replace 'e' with 'ol'. Example: Methane becomes methanol.

subject: Chemistry
text: Determining Parent Chain The longest carbon chain is the parent chain. Start numbering from the end closest to the hydroxyl group.
text_html:
Determining Parent Chain
The longest carbon chain is the parent chain. Start numbering from the end closest to the hydroxyl group.

subject: Chemistry
text: Position of Substituents The positions of the -OH group and other substituents are denoted by the numbers of carbon atoms they're attached to.
text_html:
Position of Substituents
The positions of the -OH group and other substituents are denoted by the numbers of carbon atoms they're attached to.

subject: Chemistry
text: Naming Polyhydric Alcohols When naming polyhydric alcohols, keep the 'e' in the alkane and add 'ol' with a prefix like di, tri for multiple -OH groups.
text_html:
Naming Polyhydric Alcohols
When naming polyhydric alcohols, keep the 'e' in the alkane and add 'ol' with a prefix like di, tri for multiple -OH groups.

subject: Chemistry
text: Example of Polyhydric Alcohol HO-CH 2 -CH 2 -OH is named as ethane-1,2-diol, showcasing two hydroxyl groups.
text_html:
Example of Polyhydric Alcohol
HO-CH2-CH2-OH is named as ethane-1,2-diol, showcasing two hydroxyl groups.

subject: Chemistry
text: Table 7.1 Reference Refer to Table 7.1 for examples of common and IUPAC names of various alcohols.
text_html:
Table 7.1 Reference
Refer to Table 7.1 for examples of common and IUPAC names of various alcohols.

subject: Chemistry
text: Cyclic Alcohols Naming Named with the prefix cyclo and the -OH group is always attached at C-1.
text_html:
Cyclic Alcohols Naming
Named with the prefix cyclo and the -OH group is always attached at C-1.

subject: Chemistry
text: Naming Conventions Single -OH groups replace 'e' with 'ol'. Use numerical locants for positions of -OH groups and substituents.
text_html:
Naming Conventions
  • Single -OH groups replace 'e' with 'ol'.
  • Use numerical locants for positions of -OH groups and substituents.

subject: Chemistry
text: Visual Representation Refer to the images provided for graphical examples of structural and functional group positions.
text_html:
Visual Representation
Refer to the images provided for graphical examples of structural and functional group positions.

subject: Chemistry
text: Introduction to Phenols Phenols are hydroxy derivatives of benzene. The simplest form is known as phenol, both as its common name and its IUPAC name.
text_html: Introduction to Phenols Phenols are hydroxy derivatives of benzene. The simplest form is known as phenol, both as its common name and its IUPAC name.

subject: Chemistry
text: Phenol Structure Phenol contains a benzene ring in its structure.
text_html: Phenol Structure Phenol contains a benzene ring in its structure.

subject: Chemistry
text: Benzene Ring Orientation In substituted phenol compounds, the terms are used to describe the positions of the substitutes relative to each other: Ortho : 1, 2-disubstituted Meta : 1, 3-disubstituted Para : 1, 4-disubstituted
text_html:
Benzene Ring Orientation In substituted phenol compounds, the terms are used to describe the positions of the substitutes relative to each other:
  • Ortho: 1, 2-disubstituted
  • Meta: 1, 3-disubstituted
  • Para: 1, 4-disubstituted

subject: Chemistry
text: Common Names in Phenols The orientation terms (ortho, meta, and para) are used frequently when referring to common names of phenol derivatives.
text_html: Common Names in Phenols The orientation terms (ortho, meta, and para) are used frequently when referring to common names of phenol derivatives.

subject: Chemistry
text: Phenol Derivatives Substituted phenols can have one or more hydroxyl groups attached to the benzene ring.
text_html: Phenol Derivatives Substituted phenols can have one or more hydroxyl groups attached to the benzene ring.

subject: Chemistry
text: Dihydroxy Benzene Derivatives In dihydroxy benzene derivatives, these are recognized as benzenediols.
text_html: Dihydroxy Benzene Derivatives In dihydroxy benzene derivatives, these are recognized as benzenediols.

subject: Chemistry
text: Positional Naming of Dihydroxy Benzenes There are three main isomers for dihydroxy derivatives of benzene: 1, 2-benzenediol 1, 3-benzenediol 1, 4-benzenediol
text_html:
Positional Naming of Dihydroxy Benzenes There are three main isomers for dihydroxy derivatives of benzene:
  • 1, 2-benzenediol
  • 1, 3-benzenediol
  • 1, 4-benzenediol

subject: Chemistry
text: Specific Names for Dihydroxy Benzenes The common names of the key dihydroxybenzenes are catechol, resorcinol, and hydroquinone.
text_html: Specific Names for Dihydroxy Benzenes The common names of the key dihydroxybenzenes are catechol, resorcinol, and hydroquinone.

subject: Chemistry
text: Applications of Phenol Phenols are used widely in various industrial applications including the production of plastics and other chemicals.
text_html: Applications of Phenol Phenols are used widely in various industrial applications including the production of plastics and other chemicals.

subject: Chemistry
text: Chemical Reactions of Phenols Phenols showcase unique chemical properties due to the presence of the hydroxyl group.
text_html: Chemical Reactions of Phenols Phenols showcase unique chemical properties due to the presence of the hydroxyl group.

subject: Chemistry
text: Ethers Naming Convention Common names are derived from alkyl/aryl groups written alphabetically and adding 'ether'.
text_html:
Ethers Naming Convention
Common names are derived from alkyl/aryl groups written alphabetically and adding 'ether'.

subject: Chemistry
text: Common Ether Example CH3OC2H5 is called ethylmethyl ether.
text_html:
Common Ether Example
CH3OC2H5 is called ethylmethyl ether.

subject: Chemistry
text: Table Reference Refer to Table 7.2 for common and IUPAC names of ethers.
text_html:
Table Reference
Refer to Table 7.2 for common and IUPAC names of ethers.

subject: Chemistry
text: Identical Alkyl Groups When alkyl groups are identical, 'di' precedes the name. For instance: C2H5OC2H5 is diethyl ether.
text_html:
Identical Alkyl Groups
When alkyl groups are identical, 'di' precedes the name. For instance:
  • C2H5OC2H5 is diethyl ether.

subject: Chemistry
text: IUPAC Naming Ethers are seen as hydrocarbons with hydrogen atoms replaced by -OR or -OAr groups.
text_html:
IUPAC Naming
Ethers are seen as hydrocarbons with hydrogen atoms replaced by -OR or -OAr groups.

subject: Chemistry
text: Hydrocarbon Family The larger group attached to oxygen is the parent hydrocarbon.
text_html:
Hydrocarbon Family
The larger group attached to oxygen is the parent hydrocarbon.

subject: Chemistry
text: IUPAC Naming Rule The parent hydrocarbon is identified based on the larger R or Ar group.
text_html:
IUPAC Naming Rule
The parent hydrocarbon is identified based on the larger R or Ar group.

subject: Chemistry
text: Group Representation 'R' is for alkyl groups, 'Ar' is for aryl groups.
text_html:
Group Representation
'R' is for alkyl groups, 'Ar' is for aryl groups.

subject: Chemistry
text: Naming Flexibility Ethers can have names in both common and IUPAC formats.
text_html:
Naming Flexibility
Ethers can have names in both common and IUPAC formats.

subject: Chemistry
text: Nomenclature Examples Examples provided in Table 7.2 help in understanding the naming of ethers.
text_html:
Nomenclature Examples
Examples provided in Table 7.2 help in understanding the naming of ethers.

subject: Chemistry
text: Alcohols from Alkenes: Acid Catalysed Hydration Alcohols are formed by the reaction of alkenes with water, using an acid as the catalyst, following Markovnikov’s rule for unsymmetrical alkenes.
text_html:
Alcohols from Alkenes: Acid Catalysed Hydration
Alcohols are formed by the reaction of alkenes with water, using an acid as the catalyst, following Markovnikov’s rule for unsymmetrical alkenes.

subject: Chemistry
text: Mechanism of Acid Catalysed Hydration Protonation forms a carbocation via H3O+ attack. Nucleophilic attack by water on the carbocation. Deprotonation results in alcohol formation.
text_html:
Mechanism of Acid Catalysed Hydration
  • Protonation forms a carbocation via H3O+ attack.
  • Nucleophilic attack by water on the carbocation.
  • Deprotonation results in alcohol formation.

subject: Chemistry
text: Alcohols from Alkenes: Hydroboration-Oxidation Diborane reacts with alkenes forming trialkyl boranes, which oxidize to alcohols using hydrogen peroxide and sodium hydroxide, opposing Markovnikov's rule.
text_html:
Alcohols from Alkenes: Hydroboration-Oxidation
Diborane reacts with alkenes forming trialkyl boranes, which oxidize to alcohols using hydrogen peroxide and sodium hydroxide, opposing Markovnikov's rule.

subject: Chemistry
text: Alcohols from Aldehydes and Ketones Aldehydes and ketones are converted to alcohols through catalytic hydrogenation using metal catalysts like platinum, palladium, or nickel, yielding primary or secondary alcohols respectively.
text_html:
Alcohols from Aldehydes and Ketones
Aldehydes and ketones are converted to alcohols through catalytic hydrogenation using metal catalysts like platinum, palladium, or nickel, yielding primary or secondary alcohols respectively.

subject: Chemistry
text: Alcohols from Carboxylic Acids via Reduction Use lithium aluminium hydride to reduce carboxylic acids into primary alcohols, acknowledging it's an expensive reagent reserved for special chemicals.
text_html:
Alcohols from Carboxylic Acids via Reduction
Use lithium aluminium hydride to reduce carboxylic acids into primary alcohols, acknowledging it's an expensive reagent reserved for special chemicals.

subject: Chemistry
text: Alternative Reduction of Carboxylic Acids Convert acids to esters first, then reduce them using hydrogen and a catalyst to form alcohols, making commercial production feasible.
text_html:
Alternative Reduction of Carboxylic Acids
Convert acids to esters first, then reduce them using hydrogen and a catalyst to form alcohols, making commercial production feasible.

subject: Chemistry
text: Alcohols from Grignard Reagents Grignard reagents react with aldehydes or ketones to form alcohols, through nucleophilic addition and subsequent adduct hydrolysis.
text_html:
Alcohols from Grignard Reagents
Grignard reagents react with aldehydes or ketones to form alcohols, through nucleophilic addition and subsequent adduct hydrolysis.

subject: Chemistry
text: Alcohol Variations in Grignard Reactions Using Grignard reagents with different carbonyl compounds results in primary alcohols from methanal, secondary from other aldehydes, and tertiary from ketones.
text_html:
Alcohol Variations in Grignard Reactions
Using Grignard reagents with different carbonyl compounds results in primary alcohols from methanal, secondary from other aldehydes, and tertiary from ketones.

subject: Chemistry
text: Considerations in Alcohols Formation Markovnikov’s rule for unsymmetrical alkenes. Opposition in hydroboration-oxidation. Cost and utility in reduction methods.
text_html:
Considerations in Alcohols Formation
  • Markovnikov’s rule for unsymmetrical alkenes.
  • Opposition in hydroboration-oxidation.
  • Cost and utility in reduction methods.

subject: Chemistry
text: Commercial Preparation of Alcohols Employ esterification followed by hydrogenation for cost-effective alcohol production, versus direct reduction using expensive reducing agents.
text_html:
Commercial Preparation of Alcohols
Employ esterification followed by hydrogenation for cost-effective alcohol production, versus direct reduction using expensive reducing agents.

subject: Chemistry
text: Isolation of Phenol Phenol, also known as carbolic acid, was originally isolated from coal tar in the early 19th century. Today, it is produced synthetically on a commercial scale.
text_html:
Isolation of Phenol
Phenol, also known as carbolic acid, was originally isolated from coal tar in the early 19th century. Today, it is produced synthetically on a commercial scale.

subject: Chemistry
text: Methods of Preparing Phenol in the Laboratory Several methods to prepare phenol from benzene derivatives include: From haloarenes From benzenesulphonic acid From diazonium salts From cumene
text_html:
Methods of Preparing Phenol in the Laboratory
Several methods to prepare phenol from benzene derivatives include:
  • From haloarenes
  • From benzenesulphonic acid
  • From diazonium salts
  • From cumene

subject: Chemistry
text: Preparation from Haloarenes Chlorobenzene undergoes fusion with sodium hydroxide at 623K and 320 atmospheric pressure. Phenol is obtained post-acidification of the sodium phenoxide produced in the reaction.
text_html:
Preparation from Haloarenes
Chlorobenzene undergoes fusion with sodium hydroxide at 623K and 320 atmospheric pressure. Phenol is obtained post-acidification of the sodium phenoxide produced in the reaction.

subject: Chemistry
text: Result of Haloarenes Process The chemical product realized from this method ensures phenol extraction from chloro substituents positioned on benzene.
text_html:
Result of Haloarenes Process
The chemical product realized from this method ensures phenol extraction from chloro substituents positioned on benzene.

subject: Chemistry
text: Preparation from Benzenesulphonic Acid Benzene is initially sulphonated using oleum, forming benzene sulphonic acid. This substance is then treated with molten sodium hydroxide, converting it into sodium phenoxide. Final acidification reveals phenol.
text_html:
Preparation from Benzenesulphonic Acid
Benzene is initially sulphonated using oleum, forming benzene sulphonic acid. This substance is then treated with molten sodium hydroxide, converting it into sodium phenoxide. Final acidification reveals phenol.

subject: Chemistry
text: Importance of Sodium in Benzenesulphonic Acid Method The use of sodium hydroxide transforms the intermediate into a viable phenoxide salt preceding final acidification.
text_html:
Importance of Sodium in Benzenesulphonic Acid Method
The use of sodium hydroxide transforms the intermediate into a viable phenoxide salt preceding final acidification.

subject: Chemistry
text: Preparation from Diazonium Salts An aromatic amine undergoes transformation into a diazonium salt when treated with nitrous acid (NaNO2 + HCl) at 273-278 K. This diazonium salt is solvable into phenol when warmed with water or treated with dilute acids.
text_html:
Preparation from Diazonium Salts
An aromatic amine undergoes transformation into a diazonium salt when treated with nitrous acid (NaNO2 + HCl) at 273-278 K. This diazonium salt is solvable into phenol when warmed with water or treated with dilute acids.

subject: Chemistry
text: Result of Diazonium Process The method proficiently extracts phenol through hydrolysis of diazonium salts available from aromatic amines.
text_html:
Result of Diazonium Process
The method proficiently extracts phenol through hydrolysis of diazonium salts available from aromatic amines.

subject: Chemistry
text: Preparation from Cumene Phenol is synthesized commercially from cumene or isopropylbenzene, which is oxidized in the presence of air to form cumene hydroperoxide, leading to phenol and acetone production upon acid treatment.
text_html:
Preparation from Cumene
Phenol is synthesized commercially from cumene or isopropylbenzene, which is oxidized in the presence of air to form cumene hydroperoxide, leading to phenol and acetone production upon acid treatment.

subject: Chemistry
text: By-Products in Cumene Process Along with phenol, acetone is generated in ample quantities as a by-product in the cumene process.
text_html:
By-Products in Cumene Process
Along with phenol, acetone is generated in ample quantities as a by-product in the cumene process.

subject: Chemistry
text: Dehydration of Alcohols Alcohols can be dehydrated using protic acids like H2SO4 and H3PO4.
text_html: Dehydration of Alcohols Alcohols can be dehydrated using protic acids like H2SO4 and H3PO4.

subject: Chemistry
text: Products of Dehydration The reaction conditions affect whether alkenes or ethers are produced during dehydration.
text_html: Products of Dehydration The reaction conditions affect whether alkenes or ethers are produced during dehydration.

subject: Chemistry
text: Ethanol Dehydration Ethanol forms ethene in the presence of sulphuric acid at 443 K and ethoxyethane at 413 K.
text_html: Ethanol Dehydration Ethanol forms ethene in the presence of sulphuric acid at 443 K and ethoxyethane at 413 K.

subject: Chemistry
text: Ether Formation Reaction Involves nucleophilic bimolecular reaction (SN2) where an alcohol molecule attacks a protonated alcohol.
text_html: Ether Formation Reaction Involves nucleophilic bimolecular reaction (SN2) where an alcohol molecule attacks a protonated alcohol.

subject: Chemistry
text: Suitable Reaction Conditions for Ethers - Favorable for ethers if the alkyl group is primary and unhindered. - Low temperatures favor substitution producing ethers.
text_html: Suitable Reaction Conditions for Ethers - Favorable for ethers if the alkyl group is primary and unhindered. - Low temperatures favor substitution producing ethers.

subject: Chemistry
text: Secondary & Tertiary Alcohols - Dehydration favors alkenes over ethers. - Competes with substitution leading to elimination pathways.
text_html: Secondary & Tertiary Alcohols - Dehydration favors alkenes over ethers. - Competes with substitution leading to elimination pathways.

subject: Chemistry
text: Williamson Synthesis A laboratory process for making symmetrical and unsymmetrical ethers by reacting alkyl halides with sodium alkoxide.
text_html: Williamson Synthesis A laboratory process for making symmetrical and unsymmetrical ethers by reacting alkyl halides with sodium alkoxide.

subject: Chemistry
text: SN2 Pathway in Williamson Synthesis Involves SN2 attack of an alkoxide ion on a primary alkyl halide, producing ether.
text_html: SN2 Pathway in Williamson Synthesis Involves SN2 attack of an alkoxide ion on a primary alkyl halide, producing ether.

subject: Chemistry
text: Limitation with Secondary & Tertiary Alkyl Halides - Elimination competes with substitution. - Tertiary alkyl halides yield alkenes, not ethers (e.g., CH3ONa with (CH3)3C–Br results in 2-methylpropene).
text_html: Limitation with Secondary & Tertiary Alkyl Halides - Elimination competes with substitution. - Tertiary alkyl halides yield alkenes, not ethers (e.g., CH3ONa with (CH3)3C–Br results in 2-methylpropene).

subject: Chemistry
text: Phenoxide Use in Williamson Synthesis Phenols can be converted to ethers where the phenoxide ion acts as the nucleophile.
text_html: Phenoxide Use in Williamson Synthesis Phenols can be converted to ethers where the phenoxide ion acts as the nucleophile.

subject: Chemistry
text: Mechanisms: Hydration of Alkenes CH 2 -CH=CH 2 + H 2 O {tex}CH_3-\underset{OH}{\underset\vert CH}-CH_3{/tex}
text_html:
Mechanisms: Hydration of Alkenes

CH2-CH=CH2 + H2O  {tex}CH_3-\underset{OH}{\underset\vert CH}-CH_3{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
text: Alcohols and Reaction Types Alcohols can undergo two main types of bond cleavage during chemical reactions, leading to diverse products and exhibiting both acidic and electrophilic properties.
text_html:
Alcohols and Reaction Types
Alcohols can undergo two main types of bond cleavage during chemical reactions, leading to diverse products and exhibiting both acidic and electrophilic properties.

subject: Chemistry
text: O-H Bond and Acidic Characteristics The O-H bond cleavage showcases the acidic nature of alcohols. Examples of reactions include those with active metals and esterification processes.
text_html:
O-H Bond and Acidic Characteristics
  • The O-H bond cleavage showcases the acidic nature of alcohols.
  • Examples of reactions include those with active metals and esterification processes.

subject: Chemistry
text: C-O Bond Cleavage and Electrophilicity Post protonation, the C-O bond cleavage in alcohols renders the alcohol as an electrophile, leading to vital reactions such as conversions to alkyl halides.
text_html:
C-O Bond Cleavage and Electrophilicity
Post protonation, the C-O bond cleavage in alcohols renders the alcohol as an electrophile, leading to vital reactions such as conversions to alkyl halides.

subject: Chemistry
text: Conversion to Alkyl Halides with PCl₃, PCl₅, and SOCl₂ Alcohols can also react with reagents like phosphorus trichloride, phosphorus pentachloride, or thionyl chloride to produce alkyl halides.
text_html:
Conversion to Alkyl Halides with PCl₃, PCl₅, and SOCl₂
Alcohols can also react with reagents like phosphorus trichloride, phosphorus pentachloride, or thionyl chloride to produce alkyl halides.

subject: Chemistry
text: Oxidation of Alcohols Depending on the type of alcohol, primary, secondary, or tertiary, oxidation can lead to different products: Primary alcohols oxidize to aldehydes and further to carboxylic acids. Secondary alcohols change into ketones. Tertiary alcohols resist mild oxidation but break down under stronger conditions.
text_html:
Oxidation of Alcohols
Depending on the type of alcohol, primary, secondary, or tertiary, oxidation can lead to different products:
  • Primary alcohols oxidize to aldehydes and further to carboxylic acids.
  • Secondary alcohols change into ketones.
  • Tertiary alcohols resist mild oxidation but break down under stronger conditions.

subject: Chemistry
text: Summary of Key Alcohol Reactions Breaking O-H bonds to show acidity. Conversion with active metals and esterified forms. C-O bond splits facilitating conversion to alkyl halides and dehydration to alkenes. Essential reagents include HX, PCl₃, PCl₅, and SOCl₂ in various conversions.
text_html:
Summary of Key Alcohol Reactions
  • Breaking O-H bonds to show acidity.
  • Conversion with active metals and esterified forms.
  • C-O bond splits facilitating conversion to alkyl halides and dehydration to alkenes.
  • Essential reagents include HX, PCl₃, PCl₅, and SOCl₂ in various conversions.

subject: Chemistry
text: Salt Formation in Phenols Phenols are acidic and can react with bases like NaOH to form sodium phenoxide, which is a type of salt. The reaction is as follows: Phenol + NaOH → Sodium phenoxide + H 2 O
text_html:
Salt Formation in Phenols
Phenols are acidic and can react with bases like NaOH to form sodium phenoxide, which is a type of salt. The reaction is as follows:
  • Phenol + NaOH → Sodium phenoxide + H2O

subject: Chemistry
text: Esterification in Phenols Phenols can form phenyl esters by reacting with acid chlorides or acid anhydrides. An example of such a reaction is: Phenol + Acetyl chloride → Phenyl acetate + HCl
text_html:
Esterification in Phenols
Phenols can form phenyl esters by reacting with acid chlorides or acid anhydrides. An example of such a reaction is:
  • Phenol + Acetyl chloride → Phenyl acetate + HCl

subject: Chemistry
text: Ether Formation (Williamson Ether Synthesis) Sodium phenoxide, when reacted with alkyl halides, produces ethers. This process involves: Sodium phenoxide + Methyl iodide → Anisole (Methoxybenzene) + NaI
text_html:
Ether Formation (Williamson Ether Synthesis)
Sodium phenoxide, when reacted with alkyl halides, produces ethers. This process involves:
  • Sodium phenoxide + Methyl iodide → Anisole (Methoxybenzene) + NaI

subject: Chemistry
text: Nitration of Phenols Phenols can undergo nitration resulting in different products: With dilute nitric acid, a mixture of ortho- and para-nitrophenols is formed. Concentrated nitric acid yields 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (picric acid).
text_html:
Nitration of Phenols
Phenols can undergo nitration resulting in different products:
  • With dilute nitric acid, a mixture of ortho- and para-nitrophenols is formed.
  • Concentrated nitric acid yields 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (picric acid).

subject: Chemistry
text: Halogenation (Bromination) The hydroxyl group enhances the reactivity of the benzene ring. Phenol reacts with bromine water to form 2,4,6-tribromophenol without requiring a Lewis acid.
text_html:
Halogenation (Bromination)
The hydroxyl group enhances the reactivity of the benzene ring. Phenol reacts with bromine water to form 2,4,6-tribromophenol without requiring a Lewis acid.

subject: Chemistry
text: The Kolbe Reaction This reaction is used to convert phenol into salicylic acid. The sequence involves the following steps: Phenol → Sodium phenoxide Reaction with CO 2 under pressure → Sodium salicylate Acidification → Salicylic acid
text_html:
The Kolbe Reaction
This reaction is used to convert phenol into salicylic acid. The sequence involves the following steps:
  1. Phenol → Sodium phenoxide
  2. Reaction with CO2 under pressure → Sodium salicylate
  3. Acidification → Salicylic acid

subject: Chemistry
text: Reimer-Tiemann Reaction This reaction introduces a formyl group at the ortho position of the phenol ring. The typical reaction is: Phenol + Chloroform + NaOH → Salicylaldehyde
text_html:
Reimer-Tiemann Reaction
This reaction introduces a formyl group at the ortho position of the phenol ring. The typical reaction is:
  • Phenol + Chloroform + NaOH → Salicylaldehyde

subject: Chemistry
text: Removal of Hydroxyl Group Phenol can be reduced to benzene by using zinc dust. The reaction proceeds as follows: Phenol + Zn dust → Benzene + ZnO
text_html:
Removal of Hydroxyl Group
Phenol can be reduced to benzene by using zinc dust. The reaction proceeds as follows:
  • Phenol + Zn dust → Benzene + ZnO

subject: Chemistry
text: Electrophilic Aromatic Substitution Phenols can undergo various electrophilic aromatic substitutions due to the activation by the hydroxyl group, making them more reactive toward halogenation and nitration.
text_html:
Electrophilic Aromatic Substitution
Phenols can undergo various electrophilic aromatic substitutions due to the activation by the hydroxyl group, making them more reactive toward halogenation and nitration.

subject: Chemistry
text: Properties of Phenols Phenols are known for their acidic nature due to the presence of the hydroxyl group, allowing them to partake in reactions such as salt formation and esterification.
text_html:
Properties of Phenols
Phenols are known for their acidic nature due to the presence of the hydroxyl group, allowing them to partake in reactions such as salt formation and esterification.

subject: Chemistry
text: Physical State & Appearance Phenol is a colorless crystalline solid at room temperature. It turns pink or reddish when exposed to air due to oxidation.
text_html:
Physical State & Appearance
  • Phenol is a colorless crystalline solid at room temperature.
  • It turns pink or reddish when exposed to air due to oxidation.

subject: Chemistry
text: Odour Phenol has a distinctive medicinal smell, similar to antiseptics. It serves as the active ingredient in some traditional antiseptic solutions.
text_html:
Odour
  • Phenol has a distinctive medicinal smell, similar to antiseptics.
  • It serves as the active ingredient in some traditional antiseptic solutions.

subject: Chemistry
text: Solubility in Water Phenol is moderately soluble in water through hydrogen bonding with water molecules. Solubility decreases as the size of the hydrophobic aryl group or alkyl substituents increases.
text_html:
Solubility in Water
  • Phenol is moderately soluble in water through hydrogen bonding with water molecules.
  • Solubility decreases as the size of the hydrophobic aryl group or alkyl substituents increases.

subject: Chemistry
text: Boiling and Melting Point Phenols have higher boiling points than hydrocarbons and ethers of similar molecular mass. This is due to intermolecular hydrogen bonding between phenol molecules. Melting point of phenol is 41°C. Boiling point of phenol is 182°C.
text_html:
Boiling and Melting Point
  • Phenols have higher boiling points than hydrocarbons and ethers of similar molecular mass.
  • This is due to intermolecular hydrogen bonding between phenol molecules.
  • Melting point of phenol is 41°C.
  • Boiling point of phenol is 182°C.

subject: Chemistry
text: Acidity Phenol is more acidic than alcohols, yet less acidic than carboxylic acids. It turns blue litmus paper red, indicating weakly acidic characteristics. Resonance stabilizes the phenoxide ion formed after losing H⁺, boosting its acidity.
text_html:
Acidity
  • Phenol is more acidic than alcohols, yet less acidic than carboxylic acids.
  • It turns blue litmus paper red, indicating weakly acidic characteristics.
  • Resonance stabilizes the phenoxide ion formed after losing H⁺, boosting its acidity.

subject: Chemistry
text: Solubility in Organic Solvents Phenols are soluble in organic solvents such as alcohol, ether, and benzene.
text_html:
Solubility in Organic Solvents
  • Phenols are soluble in organic solvents such as alcohol, ether, and benzene.

subject: Chemistry
text: Color Change on Oxidation Phenol changes color due to oxidation when exposed to air, transitioning from colorless to pink or reddish.
text_html:
Color Change on Oxidation
  • Phenol changes color due to oxidation when exposed to air, transitioning from colorless to pink or reddish.

subject: Chemistry
text: Role in Antiseptic Solutions Phenol's distinctive medicinal odor makes it an essential ingredient in some antiseptic solutions and formulations.
text_html:
Role in Antiseptic Solutions
  • Phenol's distinctive medicinal odor makes it an essential ingredient in some antiseptic solutions and formulations.

subject: Chemistry
text: Intermolecular Bonding Phenols exhibit stronger intermolecular hydrogen bonding compared to ethers or hydrocarbons, leading to higher boiling points.
text_html:
Intermolecular Bonding
  • Phenols exhibit stronger intermolecular hydrogen bonding compared to ethers or hydrocarbons, leading to higher boiling points.

subject: Chemistry
text: Chemical Behavior in Water In water, phenol demonstrates hydrogen bond formation, enhancing its solubility despite its hydrophobic aryl group.
text_html:
Chemical Behavior in Water
  • In water, phenol demonstrates hydrogen bond formation, enhancing its solubility despite its hydrophobic aryl group.

subject: Chemistry
text: Classification of ethers: Simple/Symmetric: Alkyl/aryl groups attached to oxygen are same e.g. CH 3 CH 2 OCH 2 CH 3 Mixed/Unsymmetric: Two different alkyl/aryl groups attached to oxygen e.g. CH 3 OCH 2 CH 3
text_html:
Classification of ethers:
Simple/Symmetric: Alkyl/aryl groups attached to oxygen are same e.g. CH3CH2OCH2CH3
Mixed/Unsymmetric: Two different alkyl/aryl groups attached to oxygen e.g. CH3OCH2CH3

subject: Chemistry
text: Cleavage of C-O Bonds in Ethers Cleavage of the C-O bond in ethers requires harsh conditions, typically with excess hydrogen halides. The reaction will form two alkyl halide molecules when dealing with dialkyl ethers.
text_html: Cleavage of C-O Bonds in Ethers Cleavage of the C-O bond in ethers requires harsh conditions, typically with excess hydrogen halides. The reaction will form two alkyl halide molecules when dealing with dialkyl ethers.

subject: Chemistry
text: Alkyl Aryl Ethers Cleavage Alkyl aryl ethers cleave at the alkyl-oxygen bond, creating phenol and alkyl halide due to the stable aryl-oxygen bond.
text_html: Alkyl Aryl Ethers Cleavage Alkyl aryl ethers cleave at the alkyl-oxygen bond, creating phenol and alkyl halide due to the stable aryl-oxygen bond.

subject: Chemistry
text: Reactivity of Ethers The reactivity order for hydrogen halides in the cleavage of ethers is: HI > HBr > HCl The reaction occurs with concentrated HI or HBr at elevated temperatures.
text_html:
Reactivity of Ethers The reactivity order for hydrogen halides in the cleavage of ethers is:
  • HI > HBr > HCl
The reaction occurs with concentrated HI or HBr at elevated temperatures.

subject: Chemistry
text: Ether Cleavage Mechanism Ether cleavage begins with protonation by HI, forming an oxonium ion. Iodide, as a strong nucleophile, attacks the less substituted carbon by the SN2 mechanism, resulting in the formation of alkyl iodide.
text_html: Ether Cleavage Mechanism Ether cleavage begins with protonation by HI, forming an oxonium ion. Iodide, as a strong nucleophile, attacks the less substituted carbon by the SN2 mechanism, resulting in the formation of alkyl iodide.

subject: Chemistry
text: Cleavage with Tertiary Alkyl Groups If a tertiary alkyl group is present in the ether, the reaction shifts to an SN1 mechanism to form a more stable carbocation, leading to tertiary halide formation.
text_html: Cleavage with Tertiary Alkyl Groups If a tertiary alkyl group is present in the ether, the reaction shifts to an SN1 mechanism to form a more stable carbocation, leading to tertiary halide formation.

subject: Chemistry
text: Anisole Cleavage Anisole forms a methylphenyl oxonium ion upon protonation. The O-CH3 bond breaks due to its relative weakness, forming CH3I. The reaction stops with phenols as they do not undergo nucleophilic substitution.
text_html: Anisole Cleavage Anisole forms a methylphenyl oxonium ion upon protonation. The O-CH3 bond breaks due to its relative weakness, forming CH3I. The reaction stops with phenols as they do not undergo nucleophilic substitution.

subject: Chemistry
text: Electrophilic Substitution in Ethers The alkoxy group (-OR) is ortho, para directing, enhancing aromatic rings' reactivity towards electrophilic substitution, similar to activation seen in phenols.
text_html: Electrophilic Substitution in Ethers The alkoxy group (-OR) is ortho, para directing, enhancing aromatic rings' reactivity towards electrophilic substitution, similar to activation seen in phenols.

subject: Chemistry
text: Halogenation Phenylalkyl ethers undergo benzene ring bromination with bromine in ethanoic acid without the need for iron (III) bromide catalyst, achieving 90% para isomer yield due to the activation by the methoxy group.
text_html: Halogenation Phenylalkyl ethers undergo benzene ring bromination with bromine in ethanoic acid without the need for iron (III) bromide catalyst, achieving 90% para isomer yield due to the activation by the methoxy group.

subject: Chemistry
text: Friedel-Crafts Reaction Anisole participates in Friedel-Crafts reactions, permitting alkyl and acyl group introductions at ortho and para positions, with anhydrous aluminium chloride as a catalyst.
text_html: Friedel-Crafts Reaction Anisole participates in Friedel-Crafts reactions, permitting alkyl and acyl group introductions at ortho and para positions, with anhydrous aluminium chloride as a catalyst.

subject: Chemistry
text: Nitration of Ethers Anisole reacts with concentrated sulphuric and nitric acids, yielding a mixture of ortho and para nitroanisole.
text_html: Nitration of Ethers Anisole reacts with concentrated sulphuric and nitric acids, yielding a mixture of ortho and para nitroanisole.

subject: Chemistry
text: Polarity of Ethers The C-O bonds in ethers are polar, leading to a net dipole moment in the molecule.
text_html:
Polarity of Ethers
The C-O bonds in ethers are polar, leading to a net dipole moment in the molecule.

subject: Chemistry
text: Boiling Points of Ethers Ethers have boiling points comparable to alkanes of similar molecular masses, but lower than alcohols due to weak polarity.
text_html:
Boiling Points of Ethers
Ethers have boiling points comparable to alkanes of similar molecular masses, but lower than alcohols due to weak polarity.

subject: Chemistry
text: Comparison with Alcohols Alcohols have significantly higher boiling points than ethers because of hydrogen bonding.
text_html:
Comparison with Alcohols
Alcohols have significantly higher boiling points than ethers because of hydrogen bonding.

subject: Chemistry
text: Hydrogen Bonding in Alcohols The large difference in boiling points between alcohols and ethers arises from the presence of hydrogen bonding in alcohols.
text_html:
Hydrogen Bonding in Alcohols
The large difference in boiling points between alcohols and ethers arises from the presence of hydrogen bonding in alcohols.

subject: Chemistry
text: Miscibility with Water Ethers show miscibility with water similar to alcohols due to hydrogen bonding capabilities.
text_html:
Miscibility with Water
Ethers show miscibility with water similar to alcohols due to hydrogen bonding capabilities.

subject: Chemistry
text: Comparison of Miscibility Ethoxyethane: 7.5 g per 100 mL water Butan-1-ol: 9 g per 100 mL water Pentane is immiscible with water
text_html:
Comparison of Miscibility
  • Ethoxyethane: 7.5 g per 100 mL water
  • Butan-1-ol: 9 g per 100 mL water
  • Pentane is immiscible with water

subject: Chemistry
text: Hydrogen Bond With Water Similar to alcohols, the oxygen in ethers can form hydrogen bonds with water molecules.
text_html:
Hydrogen Bond With Water
Similar to alcohols, the oxygen in ethers can form hydrogen bonds with water molecules.

subject: Chemistry
text: Role of Oxygen in Ethers The oxygen atom in ethers allows for hydrogen bonding with water, resembling alcohol characteristics.
text_html:
Role of Oxygen in Ethers
The oxygen atom in ethers allows for hydrogen bonding with water, resembling alcohol characteristics.

subject: Chemistry
text: Chemical Structure Influence The molecular structure of ethers results in specific polar properties affecting physical characteristics like boiling points.
text_html:
Chemical Structure Influence
The molecular structure of ethers results in specific polar properties affecting physical characteristics like boiling points.

subject: Chemistry
text: Comparative Analysis The interactions and solubility of ethers and alcohols highlight the importance of hydrogen bonding in determining physical properties.
text_html:
Comparative Analysis
The interactions and solubility of ethers and alcohols highlight the importance of hydrogen bonding in determining physical properties.

subject: Chemistry
text: Methanol Production Methanol is primarily produced through the catalytic hydrogenation of carbon monoxide using a ZnO - Cr2O3 catalyst. The process occurs at conditions of 200-300 atm and 573-673 K.
text_html: Methanol Production Methanol is primarily produced through the catalytic hydrogenation of carbon monoxide using a ZnO - Cr2O3 catalyst. The process occurs at conditions of 200-300 atm and 573-673 K.

subject: Chemistry
text: Properties of Methanol Methanol is a colorless liquid and boils at 337 K. It is highly toxic, and ingestion can cause blindness or death. It is mainly used as a solvent in paints and varnishes.
text_html: Properties of Methanol Methanol is a colorless liquid and boils at 337 K. It is highly toxic, and ingestion can cause blindness or death. It is mainly used as a solvent in paints and varnishes.

subject: Chemistry
text: Uses of Methanol - Solvent in paints and varnishes. - Used in the production of formaldehyde.
text_html: Uses of Methanol - Solvent in paints and varnishes. - Used in the production of formaldehyde.

subject: Chemistry
text: Ethanol Production by Fermentation Ethanol is derived commercially by fermenting sugars found in molasses, sugarcane, or fruits like grapes. Fermentation employs enzymes called invertase and zymase.
text_html: Ethanol Production by Fermentation Ethanol is derived commercially by fermenting sugars found in molasses, sugarcane, or fruits like grapes. Fermentation employs enzymes called invertase and zymase.

subject: Chemistry
text: Fermentation Process In wine making, fermentation occurs when sugars and enzymes in grapes contact each other after crushing. Fermentation is anaerobic and emits carbon dioxide.
text_html: Fermentation Process In wine making, fermentation occurs when sugars and enzymes in grapes contact each other after crushing. Fermentation is anaerobic and emits carbon dioxide.

subject: Chemistry
text: Max Alcohol Concentration Zymase action stops when ethanol concentration surpasses 14%. If air mixes with the fermentation mixture, ethanol oxidizes to ethanoic acid, spoiling the flavor.
text_html: Max Alcohol Concentration Zymase action stops when ethanol concentration surpasses 14%. If air mixes with the fermentation mixture, ethanol oxidizes to ethanoic acid, spoiling the flavor.

subject: Chemistry
text: Ethanol Properties Ethanol is colorless, boiling at 351 K. It is commonly used as a solvent in paints and in synthesizing various carbon compounds.
text_html: Ethanol Properties Ethanol is colorless, boiling at 351 K. It is commonly used as a solvent in paints and in synthesizing various carbon compounds.

subject: Chemistry
text: Denaturation of Alcohol Commercial ethanol is denatured, meaning made undrinkable by adding copper sulfate for color and pyridine for a foul smell to prevent human consumption.
text_html: Denaturation of Alcohol Commercial ethanol is denatured, meaning made undrinkable by adding copper sulfate for color and pyridine for a foul smell to prevent human consumption.

subject: Chemistry
text: As Solvents: Ethers, particularly diethyl ether, serve as excellent solvents in numerous organic reactions. They are relatively inert and capable of dissolving a diverse range of organic compounds. A prominent use involves the preparation of Grignard reagents (RMgX) where ether functions as a coordinating solvent.
text_html:
As Solvents:
Ethers, particularly diethyl ether, serve as excellent solvents in numerous organic reactions. They are relatively inert and capable of dissolving a diverse range of organic compounds. A prominent use involves the preparation of Grignard reagents (RMgX) where ether functions as a coordinating solvent.

subject: Chemistry
text: As Anesthetics (Historical Use): Diethyl ether played a significant historical role as one of the first surgical anesthetics in medicine, known for its ability to induce unconsciousness. Its use has decreased due to its high flammability, as well as the potential for causing nausea and irritation.
text_html:
As Anesthetics (Historical Use):
Diethyl ether played a significant historical role as one of the first surgical anesthetics in medicine, known for its ability to induce unconsciousness. Its use has decreased due to its high flammability, as well as the potential for causing nausea and irritation.

subject: Chemistry
text: In the Manufacture of Chemicals: Ethers are integral to the synthesis of a variety of chemicals including pharmaceuticals, dyes, and perfumes. They frequently serve as intermediates in organic synthesis processes.
text_html:
In the Manufacture of Chemicals:
Ethers are integral to the synthesis of a variety of chemicals including pharmaceuticals, dyes, and perfumes. They frequently serve as intermediates in organic synthesis processes.

subject: Chemistry
text: As Fuel Additives: Compounds like methyl tert-butyl ether (MTBE) are employed as fuel additives. They function to enhance the octane number of fuel and reduce engine knocking.
text_html:
As Fuel Additives:
Compounds like methyl tert-butyl ether (MTBE) are employed as fuel additives. They function to enhance the octane number of fuel and reduce engine knocking.

subject: Chemistry
text: In Extraction Processes: Owing to their capability to dissolve non-polar substances, ethers are effectively utilized in extraction and purification processes.
text_html:
In Extraction Processes:
Owing to their capability to dissolve non-polar substances, ethers are effectively utilized in extraction and purification processes.

subject: Chemistry
text: Main Characteristics of Diethyl Ether as a Solvent: Relatively inert. Dissolves a wide range of organic compounds. Used in Grignard reagent preparation.
text_html:
Main Characteristics of Diethyl Ether as a Solvent:
  • Relatively inert.
  • Dissolves a wide range of organic compounds.
  • Used in Grignard reagent preparation.

subject: Chemistry
text: Decline of Diethyl Ether in Anesthesia: Highly flammable. Induces nausea. Causes irritation.
text_html:
Decline of Diethyl Ether in Anesthesia:
  • Highly flammable.
  • Induces nausea.
  • Causes irritation.

subject: Chemistry
text: Ethers in Chemical Synthesis: Intermediates in organic synthesis. Contribute to the production of pharmaceuticals. Used in dye and perfume manufacturing.
text_html:
Ethers in Chemical Synthesis:
  • Intermediates in organic synthesis.
  • Contribute to the production of pharmaceuticals.
  • Used in dye and perfume manufacturing.

subject: Chemistry
text: Role of Ethers in Fuel Performance: Methyl tert-butyl ether (MTBE) improves octane numbers. Contributes to reducing engine knock.
text_html:
Role of Ethers in Fuel Performance:
  • Methyl tert-butyl ether (MTBE) improves octane numbers.
  • Contributes to reducing engine knock.

subject: Chemistry
text: Application of Ethers in Extraction: Efficient in dissolving non-polar substances. Widely used in extraction and purification processes.
text_html:
Application of Ethers in Extraction:
  • Efficient in dissolving non-polar substances.
  • Widely used in extraction and purification processes.
